lesson: Distinguish leaks from bloat by diffing two heap snapshots minutes apart: unreachable growth is a leak; reachable-but-unused growth is bloat from caches or listeners never trimmed.
Tie every resource's lifetime to a stack-scoped object's destructor (RAII) so exceptions, early returns, and goto can't skip the matching free or close.
Break reference cycles in refcounted graphs by making back-pointers (child-to-parent) weak/non-owning while forward pointers stay strong, or cycles never hit zero.
Allocate short-lived batches from a bump/arena allocator and free the whole arena in one O(1) reset instead of walking objects for individual deallocation.

Separate internal fragmentation (oversized blocks from size-class rounding) from external fragmentation (free memory scattered in unusable slivers): fix the first with tighter size classes, the second with compaction or pooling.

Catch use-after-free and double-free with AddressSanitizer's poisoned redzones and quarantine: freed memory is marked unaddressable and delayed from reuse so an errant access faults immediately at the culprit line.
Bound buffer overruns with guard/redzone pages surrounding heap allocations so an out-of-bounds write segfaults at the fault site instead of silently corrupting adjacent allocator metadata.
Detect stale handles into a recycled pool slot with generation counters: reuse bumps the generation so old (index, generation) handles fail comparison instead of aliasing the new occupant.

Avoid full-heap GC pauses by exploiting the generational hypothesis: collect the young generation frequently and cheaply, promote survivors, and scan the old generation rarely using write barriers to track cross-generation pointers.
